coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as full-color and stimulus responsive fluorescence ink for information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jingwen
Zheng, Yanyan
Pang, Youyou
Chen, Jie
Zhang, Zheye
Xi, Fengna
Chen, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Developing anti-counterfeiting technique with rich information encrypted by cheap, eco-friendly and stable photoluminescent materials is useful yet challenging. Herein, we present the application of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for full-color and stimuli-responsive information encryption. GQDs with red, green and blue (RGB) fluorescence or ion-responsiveness are prepared based on scalable bottom-up molecular fusion in one-step hydrothermal or solvothermal reaction. Printable fluorescent ink is easily prepared by dispersing GQDs homogeneously in glycerol-containing ethanol. Utilizing the color, pattern, characteristic fluorescence emission spectrum, or stimuli-responsive emission of GQDs, information encryption under daylight and information decryption under UV light are realized. Moreover, N and S co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs), which have Cu2+-quenchable and cysteine (Cys)-recoverable blue fluorescence, are mixed with lanthanide complex (Eu3+DP) to realize order-sensitive stimulus-responsive information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Information encryption
Anti-counterfeiting
Fluorescence ink
Stimuli-response

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Inkjet printing or hand-writing using fluorescent ink
Synthesis and characterizations of RGB GQDs
RGB GQDs as full-color fluorescence ink for information encryption
Synthesis and characterizations of ion-responsive N, S-GQDs
Stimulus-responsive encryption based on N,S-GQDs ink
Dual and order-sensitive stimuli induced decryption by combing N,S-GQDs and lanthanide complex

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation (A) of red, green, and blue GQDs and their application as fluorescent RGB inks for inkjet printing (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A–C) TEM and HRTEM (insets) images of R-GQDs, G-GQDs and B-GQDs. (D–F) AFM images of R-GQDs, G-GQDs and B-GQDs. Inserts are the corresponding height profiles along the indicated lines.

Fluorescence (FL) excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra of R-GQDs (A), G-GQDs (B) or B-GQDs (C). The increase step of excitation wavelength is 10 nm. Insets present the photographs of solutions under visible (left) or 365 nm UV (right) light. (D–F) The relative FL ratio of R-GQDs, G-GQDs and B-GQDs under ultraviolet irradiation (365 nm, D), room temperature storage (E), or in the presence of different concentration of NaCl (F). Error bars in D-F represent the standard deviation forthree experiments.

(A) The photographs of solutions mixed in different proportions of R-GQDs, G-GQDs or B-GQDs under 365 nm UV light. (B) FL emission spectra of solutions in A. (C) The designed patterns (left) and the printed patterns under daylight (middle) or 365 nm UV light (right). (D) FL emission spectra of printed GQDs patterns (indicated as a-f in C) under 365 nm UV light.

Schematic illustration (left) for information encryption and the corresponding photographs of handwritten “GQDs” (right) under visible (top) or UV light (365 nm) using N,S-GQDs (A) or N,S-GQDs + Cu2+ (B).
Photographs (A and B, under 365 nm UV light) and schematic illustration (C) of information encryption using N,S-GQDs-Cu2+ and Eu3+DP with different spray order of CA and Cys.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as full-color and stimulus responsive fluorescence ink for information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical & Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 70 Nanyang Drive, 637457, Singapore
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical & Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 70 Nanyang Drive, 637457, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing anti-counterfeiting technique with rich information encrypted by cheap, eco-friendly and stable photoluminescent materials is useful yet challenging. Herein, we present the application of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for full-color and stimuli-responsive information encryption. GQDs with red, green and blue (RGB) fluorescence or ion-responsiveness are prepared based on scalable bottom-up molecular fusion in one-step hydrothermal or solvothermal reaction. Printable fluorescent ink is easily prepared by dispersing GQDs homogeneously in glycerol-containing ethanol. Utilizing the color, pattern, characteristic fluorescence emission spectrum, or stimuli-responsive emission of GQDs, information encryption under daylight and information decryption under UV light are realized. Moreover, N and S co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs), which have Cu2+-quenchable and cysteine (Cys)-recoverable blue fluorescence, are mixed with lanthanide complex (Eu3+DP) to realize order-sensitive stimulus-responsive information encryption.

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeiting is increasingly a global problem that widely affects governments, businesses and consumers and leads to enormous financial loss and health risks [1–5]. Anti-counterfeiting techniques that are difficult to duplicate yet easy to implement are therefore highly desired [1–6]. Among the current anti-counterfeiting methods (e.g. bar-coding, watermarking, plasmonic labels, magnetic labels), fluorescent anti-counterfeiting has attracted considerable interests owing to its concealability and ease of handling [6–10]. Various fluorescent materials, including rare-earth quantum dots [2], supramolecular nanoparticles [5], polymer dots [6], perovskite nanocrystals [9] and semiconductor quantum dots [11] have been employed for anti-counterfeiting. However, the current fluorescent anti-counterfeiting usually relies only on one specific emission wavelength, which can be easily imitated by another fluorescent material. In addition, the commonly used fluorescent materials often suffer from high cost, toxicity, or poor photostability. Hence, anti-counterfeiting with rich information encryption by cheap, eco-friendly and stable fluorescent material is desired.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or 0D graphene materials are atomically thin and nanometer wide planar carbon structures [12–17], which are the new and appealing addition to the nanocarbon materials family. Owing to the unique properties including quantum confinement induced bandgap opening (thus fluorescent), highly tunable chemicophysical properties, biocompatibility, good dispersibility, molecular size, high chemical and photo-stability [15–22], GQDs have attracted growing interests for various applications, such as, bio-imaging [17,23], sensing [18,24–30], catalysis [31–35], energy storage and conversion [36], and photoelectronic devices [37]. The unique merits of GQDs make them promising for fluorescence-based information encryption. Influenced by size, functional groups, and dopants, GQDs possess a wide range of interesting properties. Thus, they offer vast opportunities for complex and smart information encryption.

In this work, we demonstrate the first application of graphene quantum dots for full-color and stimuli-responsive information encryption. Printable anti-counterfeiting strategy is presented based on GQDs with red, green, or blue (RGB) fluorescence, which are synthesized using one-step and scalable bottom-up molecular fusion in hydrothermal or solvothermal processes (Fig. 1 ). Moreover, dual and order-sensitive stimuli induced decryption is also realized by combining Cu2+-responsive N and S co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs) with another fluorescence material (lanthanide complex). Utilizing the color, pattern, characteristic emission spectrum, or stimuli-responsiveness of fluorescent GQDs, information encryption under daylight and information decryption under UV light are achieved. Our method is of low-cost, yet ensures high security level.

Pyrene, p-phenylenediamine, NaOH, alanine (Ala), aspartic acid (Asp), glutamic acid (Glu), histidine (His), glycine (Gly), l-leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), l-threonine (Thr), l-tyrosine (Tyr), l-tryptophan (Trp), and valine (Val) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). All reagents are of analytical grade and used without further treatment. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Milli-Q, Millipore).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigation was conducted on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan) to investigate the size and crystallinity of GQDs (on ultrathin carbon film). The operating voltage is 200 kV. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were captured by Bruker Multimode 8 (Bruker. Inc, USA) using tapping mode (on freshly exfoliated mica). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to investigate chemical composition of GQDs (on Si substrate) with PHI5300 electron spectrometer via 250 W, 14 kV, Mg Ká radiation (PE Ltd., USA). Fluorescence spectra of GQDs were recorded on an RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). FL 3C-11 spectrofluorometer (Hariba Scientific, USA) was applied to determine the absolute photoluminescence quantum yields of GQDs. The fluorescence spectra were acquired by a fiber spectrometer (QE pro, ocean optics, USA) with a 365 nm UV excitation.

GQDs were synthesized using one-step hydrothermal or solvothermal processes according to the literature with modifications [24,29,38,39]. Red GQDs (R-GQDs) were prepared from p-phenylenediamine (50 mg) and dimethylformamide (DMF, 10 mL) at 180 °C for 4 h under solvothermal condition. Green GQDs (G-GQDs), blue GQDs (B-GQDs) and N,S co-doped GQDs (N,S-GQDs) were synthesized using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (2 mg/mL) as the precursor. G-GQDs were synthesized in NaOH solution (0.125 M) at 200 °C for 2 h under hydrothermal condition. B-GQDs were prepared in triethylamine at 180 °C for 4 h. N,S-GQDs were synthesized in DMF containing thiourea (15 mg/mL) and NaOH (10 mM) at 200 °C for 10 h under solvothermal condition. The organic solvents used in the synthesis processes were removed after synthesis reaction by rotary evaporation. GQD products were dialyzed in a dialysis bag (with cut-off molecular weight of 500 Da) for 24 h to remove un-reacted small molecules, which was followed by vacuum freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inkjet printing or hand-writing using fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of fluorescent GQD inks, each GQD sample was dissolved in ethanol containing glycerol (v:v = 3:1) under ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. After the as-prepared fluorescent inks was encapsulated into the ink cartridge of inkjet printer (HP DeskJet 2131) or pen, printing or hand-writing on A4 filter paper was performed. The concentration of GQDs was 10 mg/mL for printing and 2 mg/mL for hand-writing, respectively. The composite fluorescent ink for hand-writing contained N,S-GQDs (2 mg/mL) and europium (III)-dibenzoylmethane-phenanthroline hydrochloride complex (Eu3+DP, 2 mg/mL). For dual stimuli induced decryption, Cu2+ (0.5 mM) were added to N,S-GQDs-Eu3+DP ink before writing, followed by spraying of cysteine (5 mM) or citric acid (5 mM) solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of RGB GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs can be synthesized using “top-down” or “bottom-up” methods [15,16,20]. The former involves cutting the large graphitized carbon materials (e.g. graphene, carbon black, carbon nanotubes) via chemical, electrochemical, or physical approaches [26]. The latter is realized by fusion of small organic molecules under hydrothermal/solvothermal condition or pyrolysis/carbonization of organic precursors [29]. In comparison with top-down strategy, bottom-up synthesis of GQDs usually offers higher production yield, better homogeneity and ease to tailor the structure and properties (e.g., by introducing heteroatom doping, adjusting reaction time and precursor concentration). Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation of GQDs with red, green, or blue fluorescence using bottom-up methods and their use as fluorescent RGB inks for inkjet printing. For red GQDs, we used lower reaction temperature comparing to the previous report (180 vs. 200 °C) in order to obtain smaller size R-GQDs [38]. For green GQDs, we used lower precursor concentration (2 vs. 5 mg/mL) comparing to the previous report in order to obtain smaller and only single-layered GQDs [29]. The one-step bottom-up synthesis exhibits high production yield of 52% for R-GQDs, 85% for G-GQDs, and 88% for B-GQDs, respectively.

As uncovered by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 2 A–C), GQDs are rather uniform in size (on average ~4.1 nm for R-GQDs, ~3.0 nm for G-GQDs and ~3.1 nm for B-GQDs). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) reveals good crystalline with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to that of graphene (1120) plane (insets in Fig. 2A–C). The thicknesses (0.8 nm–0.9 nm) characterized by atomic force microscope (AFM, Fig. 2B–D) indicate that all these GQDs are single layered. Our R-GQDs are smaller and G-GQDs are smaller and thinner than those previously synthesized using similar protocols [29,38]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirms N doping in R-GQDs (pyridine N at 398.4 eV in high-resolution N1s spectrum [38]) and the abundance of polar groups (NH2 at 399.6 eV in high-resolution N1s spectrum [29], CO and CO at 532.0 eV and 533.8 eV in high-resolution O1s spectrum, respectively) (Fig. S1). G-GQDs contain abundant OH groups (Fig. S2). B-GQDs contain doped N atoms in the skeleton of graphene (pyrrolic N [24]), OH groups, quaternary N [24] and NO2 (Fig. S3) [29]. As seen, different synthesis methods, precursors and co-reactants lead to different chemical compositions and sizes of GQDs.

These GQDs disperse homogeneously and stably in water owing to the abundant polar groups, and emit bright red, green, or blue fluorescence respectively under 365 nm UV excitation (Insets of Fig. 3 A–C). This indicates the possibility for multi-fluorescence anti-counterfeiting using single excitation light source. The maximum emission wavelength is 600 nm for R-GQDs (Fig. 3A), 540 nm for G-GQDs (Fig. 3B), 430 nm for B-GQDs (Fig. 3C). As seen, the photoluminescence properties of GQDs is determined by their size and chemical groups resulting from specific synthetic routes. In addition, these emission peaks are independent to excitation wavelength (Fig. 3A–C), indicating the uniform size and surface states of these GQDs. Also in support of this notion, the emission spectra of these GQDs are narrow with half-peak width of ~70 nm for R-GQDs, ~80 nm for G-GQDs and ~55 nm for B-GQDs, respectively. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yields were measured to be 24.2% for R-GQDs, 19.7% for G-GQDs and 20.2% for B-GQDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3D–F, the stability of these RGB GQDs is evidenced by their unaltered fluorescence emission under the challenges by three different conditions including continuous exposure to UV light irradiation (up to 3 h), long-term storage at indoor environment (up to 30 days), and in presence of salt (up to 0.5 M). Such high stability is critical for practical use. Obviously, these unique fluorescent characteristics of GQDs will further extend the various applications of carbonaceous materials [40–43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: RGB GQDs as full-color fluorescence ink for information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 A demonstrates continuous emission of GQDs in the full color range under UV light (365 nm) enabled simply by mixing of different GQD types at different ratios. The corresponding fluorescence emission spectra are composed of the characteristic emission of mixed GQDs (Fig. 4B). Owing to the good dispersibility, GQD inks can be easily made for full-color pattern printing (Fig. 1B). Because of the non-toxic, polar and volatile characteristics, ethanol is adopted as the solvent to disperse GQDs. In addition, glycerol is added to increase the viscosity of the ink to facilitate printing. The GQD inks (10 mg/mL) can be stored in ambient for months without observing obvious precipitates or changes in optical characteristics. Fluorescent GQD inks can be loaded into the standard cartridge of a inkjet printer and be printed on papers or other flexible substrates. Because of their molecular size, GQDs will never jam the printer nozzle (~10 μm).

An image with simple pattern and two complex images encoded with different fluorescence colors are designed on the computer as the input instructions to the printer for printing on non-fluorescent papers (Fig. 4C). Under ambient room lighting, the printed images are lightly visible and essentially colorless. Upon UV illumination, the colorful images which are identical to the designs become clearly visible, demonstrating information encoding with fluorescence color and pattern. As revealed in Fig. 4D, information can also be encrypted by the characteristic fluorescence spectra from GQDs. The signature of broad GQD emission which is highly dependent on its chemical composition, dopants, size and morphology is advantageous for information encryption as compared to sharp emission from other fluorophores (e.g., semiconductor quantum dots). The rich emission features, small size, and high dispersibility of GQDs make them potentially useful to be blended into plastics, fuels, lubricants, chemicals, identifications for anti-counterfeiting purpose. Our multi-color GQD printing method substantially increases the difficulty of duplication. Note that, the fluorescent colors in printed images differ to the designed color inputs to some extent. This is because the algorithm to determine RGB ratio used by the printer are particularly calibrated for the commercial RGB cartridges. Expectedly, proper calibration determined for GQD RGB inks will lead to large improvement. In addition, smaller size of GQDs than other inorganic nanoparticles (e.g., metal–organic frameworks) used for inks might ensure higher resolution [44]. In comparison to the inks based on organic fluorophores, GQD ink printed images are much more stable (180 min UV illumination doesn’t bleach the fluorescence as shown in Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of ion-responsive N, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs with a large spectrum of properties can be synthesized using various synthetic routes. Some GQDs can specifically interact with metal ions through their functional groups or heteroatom dopants. Such interactions cause fluorescence quenching through aggregation or charge transfer induced mechanism [24–26]. This phenomenon can be utilized to devise stimuli-responsive anti-counterfeiting applications. As a proof-of-concept demonstration, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped GQDs (N,S-GQDs) were synthesized through one-step bottom-up molecular fusion between 1,3,6-trinitropyrene using thiourea as S source and N,N-dimethylformamide as both N/S source and solvent under alkaline solvothermal condition (Fig. S5) [24], with a production yield is as high as 86%. As shown in Fig. S6, the obtained N,S-GQDs exhibits small size (average ~2.9 nm), high crystallinity, and single-layer thickness. XPS investigation reveals the presence of –OH groups, CS(Ox)C (x = 2, 3 and 4) sulphonyl bridges, amide (N-CO), graphitic N, and pyrrolic N [24], indicating N, S co-doping and abundant surface groups (Fig. S7). N,S-GQDs exhibit excitation-independent blue fluorescence emission (quantum yield of 22.2%) with maximum peak at 447 nm and narrow half peak width of ~55 nm (Fig. S8A). It is known that Cu2+ ions can bind with electron-donating elements (e.g. N) through coordination interaction [24,45].

As shown in Fig. S8B, the fluorescence of N,S-GQDs is strongly quenched by Cu2+ owing to the aggregation of GQDs caused by Cu2+ coordination. Hard and soft acids and bases theory (HSAB) may be applied to explain the interaction between GQDs (base) and Cu2+ (acid) [46,47]. Doping of N and S, together with oxygen moieties, increases the surface electron density, thus making GQD a hard base. Therefore, GQDs have fast and strong reaction with hard acid Cu2+ [24]. And the quenching is largely relieved by cysteine (Cys, (R)-2-amino-3-mercaptopropanoic acid) which possesses high affinity towards Cu2+ because of amine groups [48,49], and causes release of N,S-GQDs through competitive binding with Cu2+ ions. Other amino acids do not have such capability (Fig. S8C) and Cys-induced fluorescence recovery reaches a plateau at ~3 min (Fig. S8D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stimulus-responsive encryption based on N,S-GQDs ink
ce:section.ce:para: GQD inks can not only be printed but also be written using a fountain pen. As shown in Fig. 5 A, word “GQDs” is written on a paper with N,S-GQD ink, which is completely invisible under daylight. It becomes fluorescently visible upon UV illumination. This simple encryption is convenient. When N,S-GQD ink with addition of Cu2+ ions was used, decryption was only possible by both UV illumination and spraying of Cys solution (Fig. 5B). Such stimuli-responsive encryption enhances the security level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual and order-sensitive stimuli induced decryption by combing N,S-GQDs and lanthanide complex
ce:section.ce:para: To bring this technique to the next level of complexity, we further demonstrate order-sensitive multistimuli-responsive encryption by combining N,S-GQDs with a lanthanide complex (europium (III)-dibenzoylmethane-phenanthroline hydrochloride complex-Eu3+DP, Fig. S9). Under 535 nm excitation, Eu3+DP gives red fluorescence emission peaked at 615 nm (Fig. S10A). Its fluorescence can be quenched by citric acid (CA) due to the ligand (DP) dissociation under acidic condition (inset in Fig. S10A). Hand-writing using Eu3+DP ink, which is invisible under daylight, appears red under UV light and disappears upon spraying of CA solution (Fig. S10B). This experiment demonstrates a realization of stimuli-responsive encryption enabled by Eu3+DP.

The composite ink made by mixing N,S-GQDs, Cu2+, and Eu3+DP was used to write word “GQDs” (Fig. 6 A). Red fluorescence from Eu3+DP is observed under UV light while the blue fluorescence of N,S-GQDs is quenched of by Cu2+. When CA is sprayed, the red word completely disappears under UV light because the fluorescence from Eu3+DP is quenched by CA. Subsequent spraying of Cys leads to recovery of blue fluorescence from N,S-GQDs. When the stimulus sequence changes (spraying Cys then CA), the decryption sequence becomes red → pink → blue. The pink fluorescence is resulted from the mixture of red fluorescence from Eu3+DP and recovered blue fluorescence from N,S-GQDs. Such multi-color encoding (red, dark, pink, blue) decrypted by order-sensitive sequential stimuli imposes daunting difficulty to counterfeiting (Fig. 6B).

In summary, we have demonstrated the first application of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for printable, full-color and stimuli-responsive anti-counterfeiting. Conveniently, rich information encryption under daylight and decryption under UV light are realized based on color, pattern, characteristic emission spectrum, or stimuli-responsiveness of fluorescent GQDs. Comparing with other fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials (e.g. metal–organic frameworks or semiconductor quantum dots) [11,44], carbonaceous GQDs have unique advantages, such as environmental friendliness, low-cost, tunable and stimulus-responsive photoluminescence, high photostability, and suitability to make inks due to good dispersibility and molecular size. Moreover, different to those fluorescence materials with sharp emission at a specific wavelength, the characteristic emission spectrum of GQDs, which is specifically dependent on the synthetic route, is difficult to be duplicated. As GQD family covers a wide range of chemicophysical properties, GQDs therefore allow vast opportunities for novel anti-counterfeiting designs or other information encryption purposes. For example, smart anti-counterfeiting strategies can be devised based on GQDs with photoluminescence sensitive to various ions [50], pH [51], or organic compounds [18], or GQDs with up-conversion photoluminescence [52]. Moreover, GQD inks may be useful for other applications, such as, printable sensors and printable circuits.

Jingwen Zhao: Methodology, Data curation. Yanyan Zheng: Data curation. Youyou Pang: Visualization, Data curation. Jie Chen: Methodology. Zheye Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Fengna Xi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Peng Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. : .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Ministry of Education of Singapore (AcRF Tier 2 grant MOE2017-T2-2-005), Agency for Science, Technology and Research (A*STAR) of Singapore (AME-IRG grant AMEIRG18-0016), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY19B050008) and the Fundamental Research Funds of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University ZSTU (2020Q044).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.06.077.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable solid-state fluorescent materials for supramolecular encryption

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Hou, C. Ke, C.J. Bruns, P.R. McGonigal, R.B. Pettman, J.F. Stoddart, Tunable solid-state fluorescent materials for supramolecular encryption, Nat. Commun. 6 (1) (2015) 6884.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Future prospects of luminescent nanomaterial based security inks: from synthesis to anti-counterfeiting applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Kumar, S. Singh, B.K. Gupta, Future prospects of luminescent nanomaterial based security inks: from synthesis to anti-counterfeiting applications, Nanoscale 8 (30) (2016) 14297-14340.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrophilic doped quantum dots “ink” and their inkjet-printed patterns for dual mode anticounterfeiting by reversible cation exchange mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Huang, M. Xu, J. Liu, J. Wang, Y. Zhu, J. Liu, H. Rong, J. Zhang, Hydrophilic doped quantum dots “ink” and their inkjet-printed patterns for dual mode anticounterfeiting by reversible cation exchange mechanism, Adv. Funct. Mater. 29 (17) (2019) 1808762.
S. Kalytchuk, Y. Wang, K. Poláková, R. Zbořil, Carbon dot fluorescence-lifetime-encoded anti-counterfeiting, ACS Appl. Mat. Interfaces 10 (35) (2018) 29902-29908.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color tunable fluorescent and chemiluminescent supramolecular nanoparticles for anti-counterfeiting inks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zuo, W. Qian, T. Li, X.-Y. Hu, J. Jiang, L. Wang, Full-color tunable fluorescent and chemiluminescent supramolecular nanoparticles for anti-counterfeiting inks, ACS Appl. Mat. Interfaces 10 (45) (2018) 39214-39221.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual colorimetric and fluorescent authentication based on semiconducting polymer dots for anticounterfeiting applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.K. Tsai, Y.S. Lai, P.J. Tseng, C.H. Liao, Y.H. Chan, Dual colorimetric and fluorescent authentication based on semiconducting polymer dots for anticounterfeiting applications, ACS Appl. Mat. Interfaces 9 (36) (2017) 30918-30924.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cooperative supramolecular polymers with anthracene-endoperoxide photo-switching for fluorescent anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Gao, Y. Han, F. Wang, Cooperative supramolecular polymers with anthracene-endoperoxide photo-switching for fluorescent anti-counterfeiting, Nat. Commun. 9 (1) (2018) 3977.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excellent thermal and photo stability applied as invisible ink for loading important information and anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, L. Zhou, Y. Li, R. Deng, H. Zhang, Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excellent thermal and photo stability applied as invisible ink for loading important information and anti-counterfeiting, Nanoscale 9 (2) (2017) 491-496.
